title: Ledger Entry
description: A single decision-ledger entry produced by a federation-compliant Mac tool (e.g. mac-app-audit, FreeUp Space). One ledger file is a JSON array of these.

schema_version.description: Semver of this schema the entry was written against, e.g. '1.0.0'.

entry_id.description: Stable unique identifier for this entry. Recommended: deterministic hash of producer+subject.identifier so re-runs update rather than duplicate.

producer.description: Short slug of the tool that wrote this entry, e.g. 'mac-app-audit', 'freeup-space'.

producer_version.description: Version of the producing tool, for debugging drift over time.

created_at.description: ISO 8601 UTC timestamp when this entry was first created.

updated_at.description: ISO 8601 UTC timestamp of the most recent re-evaluation. Omit on first write.

identifier.description: Stable machine identifier if one exists (bundle ID, brew formula name, absolute path, model hash). Prefer this over 'name' for entry_id derivation.

verdict.description: The producer's finding. 'do_not_touch' and 'not_assessed' are always allowed to carry a lighter evidence bar than affirmative verdicts.

summary.description: One or two sentences grounding the verdict. No bare percentages without a method backing them.

confidence_percent.description: Optional. Only meaningful when method is usage_telemetry, dependency_graph, or filesystem_stat (i.e. backed by real numbers). Omit rather than guess for manual/heuristic methods.

evidence.description: Receipts backing the verdict. May be empty only when verdict is 'not_assessed'.

value.description: Optional raw value backing the claim (a number, date string, etc). Left untyped intentionally.

reason.description: User's stated reason, especially valuable on 'rejected' — this is what lets a tool learn revealed preferences instead of re-suggesting the same thing.

revisit_after.description: If status is 'deferred', the date after which this entry should resurface.

related_entries.description: entry_ids this entry references — redundancy-cluster siblings within the same producer, or cross-product seam links.
